story:
Ethan finally does it.

In a cluttered university lab, after another sleepless night, he watches the timer over the travel chamber count down from three minutes. His theory—really, Sara’s theory—says the conscious mind can slip free, leaving the body’s autonomic systems to keep things running, then snap back after the allotted time. In principle, the slip can run backward or forward along the subject’s own timeline; backward jumps are simply the only ones they’ve dared test. When the clock hits zero, the rat he calls Bruno jolts up, grooms, scurries. Vitals stable. Proof.

He’s still grinning when the door clicks. Not the professor—Sara. Curly hair, thick frames, shoulders tense. He starts to say they did it, that her idea worked, but she presses a Ziploc into his hands. Three pregnancy tests, all marked positive. She’s keeping it. The words hollow him. He flails for delay, for logic, for better timing. She leaves in tears.

He stares at the bag. He loves her—he’d say so if he believed love was more than chemistry—but this is the worst possible moment. Then a single thought cuts through: he has a time machine.

Coffee spills over the keyboard. He wipes it up, double-checks inputs, adds buffer. Fifteen minutes into the past. Consciousness only. No Fly-style accidents. He snags Bruno, shuts him in the cage, sprints to the chamber, and blinks three times at the light.

He’s back in his chair, holding the bag, facing Sara mid-sentence. The miracle overwhelms him—then he remembers why he came. He blurts about time travel at the worst possible second. She recoils. In a blink, he’s on the chamber floor, retching, head pounding, vitals spiking but stable. Side effects: manageable.

Next plan: jump roughly three months back and preempt the mess. Send a clean text. Own up. Or lie, if he has to. He tells himself it’s temporary, a fix for terrible timing. He programs the date, fights sticky keys, starts the three-minute countdown, hurries to the chamber. Out of the corner of his eye, Bruno wriggles free, skitters across the keyboard. Ethan shouts. A directional icon on the screen flips from minus to plus before the timer hits zero. Darkness.

A girl screams. He screams back. A flashlight throws soft light across a bedroom and a kid with a halo of brown curls in pink princess pajamas. She calls him Daddy. The word hits harder than the nausea. Her name is Maya—“Mommy says you picked it.” She hands him a photo: him and Sara at the zoo, her belly round, Maya on his shoulders. A future he didn’t know he wanted.

She asks for a story. He reads about a mischievous rabbit while she corrects his vocabulary—noc… something, nocturnal—and giggles at all the right parts. She nods off against his chest. “Thanks, Daddy. I love you,” she mumbles. He answers without thinking: “I love you too.”

Then the chamber floor slams back into him.

He stumbles out, races through the lab, into the night. Catches Sara at the closing doors. He’s breathless, wrecked, changed. He tells her he saw their daughter: bright, brave, real. He tells her he wants in—not out. No fake texts. No lies. No running.

Sara searches his face and finds no dodge. He takes her hand. They stand under a buzzing streetlight, two shaken people facing a future that suddenly feels bigger than equations or proof. For once, Ethan isn’t chasing time. He’s choosing it—with her, with the child they’ll raise.

And he’s ready.
